docs.title: Linux Security
docs.text: Linux security is the practice of protecting Linux systems using access control, hardening, auditing, compliance checks, and vulnerability management.

docs.title: Official Documentation
docs.text:
Red Hat Enterprise Linux Security Documentation

https://docs.redhat.com/en/documentation/red_hat_enterprise_linux

NIST SCAP

https://csrc.nist.gov/projects/security-content-automation-protocol

OpenSCAP

https://www.open-scap.org/

CIS Benchmarks

https://www.cisecurity.org/cis-benchmarks

DISA STIG

https://public.cyber.mil/stigs/

docs.title: Authentication
docs.text:
Authentication answers:

"Who are you?"

Common methods:

  • Passwords
  • SSH keys
  • Certificates
  • Multi-factor authentication

docs.title: Basic commands
docs.text:
id
who
last
Click to expand: Authentication details

Authentication verifies the identity of a user or system.

Examples:

  • Logging into Linux with a password
  • Connecting using an SSH key
  • Using LDAP or Active Directory authentication

Linux authentication is commonly managed through PAM. Authorization

Authorization answers:

"What are you allowed to do?"

Linux controls access using:

File permissions Users and groups sudo ACLs Basic commands ls -l

groups

sudo -l

Click to expand: Authorization example

Example:

A user may successfully authenticate:

User: john
Password: correct

But authorization decides:

Can john run this command?
Can john read this file?
Can john become root?
Encryption

Encryption protects information from unauthorized access.

Two main types:

Data at rest

Examples:

LUKS disk encryption Encrypted backups Data in transit

Examples:

SSH TLS HTTPS

Click to expand: Encryption commands

Check certificates:

openssl x509 -text -in certificate.crt

Test TLS:

openssl s_client -connect example.com:443
Remember

Authentication

Who are you?

Authorization

What can you do?

Encryption

Can someone read your data?

docs.title: OpenSCAP
docs.text:
OpenSCAP is an open source security compliance scanner for Linux systems.

It is used to check systems against security standards:

  • CIS Benchmarks
  • DISA STIG
  • PCI DSS
  • NIST security profiles

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
Install OpenSCAP on RHEL 
sudo dnf install openscap-scanner scap-security-guide
Verify installation 
oscap --version

docs.title: Security Content
docs.text:
OpenSCAP uses security content provided by SCAP Security Guide.

The package contains profiles for:

  • CIS
  • STIG
  • PCI DSS
  • Standard security baseline
Find available security content 
ls /usr/share/xml/scap/ssg/content/
Show available profiles 
oscap info /usr/share/xml/scap/ssg/content/ssg-rhel9-ds.xml

docs.title: Basic Scan
docs.text:
Run compliance scan 
sudo oscap xccdf eval \
--profile xccdf_org.ssgproject.content_profile_standard \
--report report.html \
/usr/share/xml/scap/ssg/content/ssg-rhel9-ds.xml

The result is an HTML report:

report.html

docs.title: Remediation
docs.text:
OpenSCAP can create remediation instructions. Ansible is commonly used to apply the fixes.

Generate Ansible remediation 
oscap xccdf generate fix \
--fix-type ansible \
results.xml > remediation.yml
Run remediation 
ansible-playbook remediation.yml

docs.title: Common Commands
docs.text:
Show version 
oscap --version
Show profile information 
oscap info file.xml
Run evaluation 
oscap xccdf eval
